mentions.verbatim: Among the efficacy-assessable patients treated at the active and tolerable doses of 800 mg o.d., 1200 mg o.d., or 600 mg b.i.d. (n = 42), objective response rate was 21.4% (95% confidence interval 10.3% to 36.8%).
Responses were observed across eight different tumor types, including squamous/non-squamous non-small-cell lung cancer, pancreatic adenocarcinoma, and biliary tract cancer.

tier_b_claims.notes: Distinct from the 80-patient all-doses safety cohort (40 mg to 1600 mg o.d. or 600 mg b.i.d.) which is the safety denominator. The 21.4% ORR refers to efficacy-assessable patients at the three active dose levels only.

display.orr_tooltip: 21.4% ORR at active doses (800 mg QD / 1200 mg QD / 600 mg BID; n=42; 95% CI 10.3–36.8%); responses across 8 tumour types incl. NSCLC, PDAC, BTC; mDOR 8.3 months. Ann Oncol 2024 / ESMO 2024 (Rodon 2024). Overall all-dose ORR lower (5/39 in dose-exploration subset).

display.detail_body_html:
📊 AMG 193 — early first-in-human responses; expanding into NSCLC dose expansion · Rodon J 2024 · also Belmontes B 2025 Sacher A 2024
  • 21.4% ORR at active doses (800 mg QD / 1200 mg QD / 600 mg BID; n=42; 95% CI 10.3–36.8%); responses across 8 tumour types including NSCLC, PDAC, biliary tract, renal, esophageal, gastric, gallbladder, ovarian Sertoli-Leydig; median DOR 8.3 months — Rodon 2024 Ann Oncol (ESMO 2024 604O). Note: all-evaluable ORR 5/39 (12.8%) includes sub-therapeutic dose cohorts; 21.4% is the clinically relevant figure at RP2D-range doses.
  • Complete tumour SDMA inhibition at ≥ 480 mg dose; ctDNA reductions > 90% observed in partial responders; myelosuppression not dose-limiting (key differentiation vs first-gen PRMT5i).
  • NSCLC dose-expansion cohort: favourable safety with no clinically significant myelosuppression reported to date, vs first-gen PRMT5i; activity reported in NSCLC, PDAC, biliary tract (WCLC 2024 P3.12D.11).
  • MTAP-loss prevalence ~16–20% in squamous NSCLC, ~12–16% in non-squamous; preferential targeting of MTA-bound PRMT5 supported in xenograft models (Belmontes 2025 Cancer Discov).

display.detail_body_html:
Summary: By incorporating a platform trial protocol design, SELECTmeso will spearhead a next-generation precision medicine trial platform in relapsed mesothelioma for the UK enabling early evaluation of hypothesis-driven targeted therapies. The SELECTmeso Platform trial protocol allows incorporation of a range of identified and yet-to-be-identified candidates as potential treatments for patients with relapsed mesothelioma into the platform. Candidate Specific Trials (CSTs) will be added into the trial via treatment-specific CSTs of this platform trial protocol as appendices. Having one platform trial protocol ensures different candidates are evaluated in the same consistent manner and that opening up new trials for new candidates is more efficient. SELECTmeso1 is our first Candidate Specific Trial (CST) on the platform. SELECTmeso1 is investigating if a drug called BMS-986504 is effective in shrinking mesothelioma tumours as a targeted treatment for patients with tumours that have specific genetic biomarkers. Biomarkers are molecules that can indicate normal or abnormal processes taking place in the body or tumour, they can indicate how effective certain treatments will be. BMS-986504 has shown to be tolerated and promising signs of activity on patients with MTAP-deficient solid tumours, in a previous mesothelioma trial.

display.detail_body_html:
Summary: People who have a cancer called MPNST, or Malignant Peripheral Nerve Sheath Tumor, may be eligible for this study. The purpose of this study is to see if a new medicine called BMS-986504 may work better than other available medicines for people with MPNST. Methylthioadenosine Phosphorylase (MTAP) loss is a gene mutation that some people have. MTAP loss seems to increase the chance that BMS-986504 can kill MPNST cancer cells. People who are missing MTAP from their tumor may be able to enroll in this study. Treating MPNST based on MTAP loss is considered experimental and is not approved by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for determining whether BMS-98650 will be active against cancer. The purpose of this study is to evaluate the safety and effectiveness of BMS-986504 in participants with MPNST.
Primary endpoint(s): Objective response rate (ORR)

display.detail_body_html:
Summary: This phase Ib/II trial tests the safety and side effects of BMS-986504 in combination with pemetrexed and how well the combination works in treating patients with solid tumors with MTAP deletion and that has spread from where it first started (primary site) to other places in the body (metastatic). The MTAP gene helps cells recycle important parts needed to make deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA), which is needed for cell growth and function. MTAP deletion means that the MTAP gene is missing. BMS-986504, a PRMT5 inhibitor, may stop the growth of tumor cells by blocking some of the enzymes needed for cell growth. Pemetrexed is in a class of medications called antifolate antineoplastic agents. It works by stopping cells from using folic acid to make DNA and may kill tumor cells. Giving BMS-986504 in combination with pemetrexed may be safe, tolerable, and/or effective in treating patients with metastatic solid tumors with MTAP deletion.

📊 MRTX1719 → BMS-986504 (navlimetostat) — currently the most advanced MTA-cooperative PRMT5i programme · Engstrom LD 2023 · also George B 2025 Rodón J 2025 Bristol Myers Squibb (presented at IASLC World Conference on Lung Cancer 2025) 2025 Aldea M 2026
  • Preclinical work reports ~70× selectivity for MTAP-null vs MTAP-WT cells (Engstrom 2023, Cancer Discov).
  • CA240-0007 phase I/II: most TRAEs grade 1–2; Grade ≥ 3 TRAEs ~13%; once-daily 400–600 mg go-forward dose supported by PK/PD (ASCO 2025 abstract 3011, George B 2025).
  • Overall across all tumour types (n=152 enrolled across all doses; DCO Dec 2, 2024; median follow-up 9.0 mo): 23% ORR, 70% DCR, 10.5-month mDOR, 4.6-month median TTR — ASCO 2025 Annual Meeting abstract 3011 (George B 2025); non-peer-reviewed.
  • Most common tumour types enrolled: NSCLC n=34, PDAC n=41, cholangiocarcinoma n=12, mesothelioma n=12.
  • NSCLC evaluable subset (n=33–35): 29–30% ORR, 80% DCR, 10.5-month mDOR; responders include EGFR-positive, ALK-positive, and squamous patients (ASCO 2025 abs. 3011; IASLC / WCLC Sep 2025; non-peer-reviewed).
  • PDAC expansion cohort (DCO Feb 7, 2025): 17% ORR, 69% DCR (ASCO 2025 abstract 3011 / WCLC 2025; non-peer-reviewed).
  • MTAP-loss prevalence in oncogene-driven NSCLC (~17–33% across ALK / RET / EGFR subsets) provides a rationale for driver-TKI combinations (Aldea 2026, Ann Oncol).
  • Pivotal Ph2/3 trials initiated in 2025–2026 (PDAC: MountainTAP-30 NCT07076121; NSCLC: MountainTAP-9 NCT06855771 with pembrolizumab + chemo).

📊 Vopimetostat + daraxonrasib — early ~92% ORR in MTAP-del RAS-mut PDAC (June 2026; small n, non-peer-reviewed) · Tango Therapeutics 2026
  • ~92% ORR (9 / 11 confirmed at data cut) in 12 evaluable MTAP-del RAS-mut metastatic PDAC patients — early, single-arm, non-peer-reviewed.

  • Tango announced intent to advance the vopimetostat + daraxonrasib combination into Phase 3 for first-line MTAP-deleted PDAC.

  • Allosteric MAT2A inhibitor; the proposed mechanism exploits the MTAP-loss-driven metabolic liability to perturb DNA replication and repair (AACR 2025 abstract 4268).
  • Combination expansion with sacituzumab govitecan (Trodelvy) in MTAP-deleted urothelial: ~33% ORR at DL1 (3 / 9), ~57% ORR at DL2 (4 / 7; 3 confirmed PR + 1 unconfirmed PR) — IDEAYA press, Sep 2025; non-peer-reviewed.
  • Reported MTAP-deletion prevalence: ~26% in urothelial, ~15% in NSCLC (~48,000 US cases annually by IDEAYA's estimate).
  • March 2026: IDEAYA announced it would deprioritize clinical activity with Gilead evaluating this combination and conclude enrollment in the Phase 1/2 trial in urothelial and lung cancers (IDEAYA Q1 2026 business update). IDE397 itself continues in IDEAYA's pipeline; a Phase 1 combination cohort pairing IDE397 with IDE892 (PRMT5i) in MTAP-deleted solid tumors is planned for mid-2026 (NCT07277413).

display.search_keywords: nct06137144 azd3470 primavera a modular phase 1/2 study to evaluate the safety tolerability and efficacy of azd3470 a protein arginine methyltransferase 5 prmt5 inhibitor in participants with relapsed/refractory hematologic malignancies classical hodgkin lymphoma chl r/r hodgkin lymphoma hematologic astrazeneca
display.detail_body_html:
📊 AZD3470 (PRIMAVERA) — MTA-cooperative PRMT5i in MTAP-silenced R/R cHL; ASCO 2026 first-in-human data · ASH 2024 abstract · also Buxton RL 2026 (J Med Chem)
  • AZD3470 is a second-generation, orally bioavailable MTA-cooperative PRMT5 inhibitor; preferentially binds PRMT5 in the presence of MTA, exploiting MTAP loss.
  • Biomarker rationale in cHL: MTAP is silenced by promoter hypermethylation in >80% of classical Hodgkin lymphoma cases — distinct from the homozygous genomic deletion seen in solid tumours, but producing the same MTA-accumulation / PRMT5 vulnerability.
  • PRIMAVERA dose-escalation (NCT06137144), 39 R/R cHL patients, 25–600 mg, median 6 prior lines (including brentuximab vedotin and anti-PD-1): 59 evaluable patients at data cut.
  • Overall ORR 44% (26/59 evaluable); at ≥450 mg (n=31): 58% ORR, 35% CR; at 600 mg (n=16): 63% ORR, 44% CR — ASCO 2026 Annual Meeting, 30 May 2026; non-peer-reviewed.
  • Safety: no DLTs, no treatment discontinuations due to AEs; 85% any TEAE; 28% Grade ≥3 (primarily neutropenia, hypokalemia); no anaemia DLT; 51% still on treatment at data cut; median exposure 15 weeks.
  • Parallel solid-tumour programme: PRIMROSE (NCT06130553), Phase I/IIa in MTAP-deficient advanced solid tumours — TPS presented ASCO 2024; dose-escalation ongoing, no efficacy data released to date.
Summary: PRIMAVERA is a modular Phase I/II dose-escalation and expansion study of AZD3470 (an MTA-cooperative PRMT5 inhibitor) as monotherapy and in combination with anticancer agents in participants with relapsed or refractory hematologic malignancies, including classical Hodgkin lymphoma where MTAP is silenced by promoter hypermethylation in >80% of cases.
Primary endpoint(s): Safety, tolerability, and preliminary efficacy (ORR) of AZD3470; dose-limiting toxicities; recommended Phase 2 dose
Interventions: AZD3470

display.detail_body_html:
Summary: First-in-human Phase I study of BAY 3713372, a second-generation brain-penetrant MTA-cooperative PRMT5 inhibitor, in participants with MTAP-deleted solid tumors. Bayer obtained worldwide rights via exclusive license from Puhe BioPharma (March 2025). Key differentiating feature is CNS penetrance, targeting GBM and CNS metastases in addition to systemic MTAP-deleted tumours. No efficacy data released to date.
Primary endpoint(s): Safety, tolerability, PK/PD, and preliminary anti-tumour activity of BAY 3713372
Interventions: BAY 3713372

display.detail_body_html:
Summary: Phase 1/2 first-in-human study of TNG456, a next-generation brain-penetrant MTA-cooperative PRMT5 inhibitor developed by Tango Therapeutics, as monotherapy and in combination with abemaciclib (CDK4/6 inhibitor) in patients with MTAP-deleted GBM and other solid tumours. First patient dosed May 21, 2025. Preclinical: 45% of GBM is MTAP-deleted; TNG456 demonstrates sufficient CNS exposure for meaningful efficacy in GBM models (AACR 2025 abstract). Phase 1 dose escalation is GBM-focused. No efficacy data released to date.
Primary endpoint(s): Safety, tolerability, PK, PD, and antitumour activity; Phase 2: ORR
Interventions: TNG456 | Abemaciclib
